vulnerabilities.title: Marketplace Freeze Prevents Emergency Unlisting Including by Admin
vulnerabilities.summary: The unlistVesting function has the isFreeze modifier, which prevents ALL callers (including s2Admin) from unlisting when the marketplace is frozen. This contradicts the emergency design intent where the admin should be able to unlist vestings during a freeze to protect user funds. Sellers' listed vesting tokens are locked indefinitely during freeze.

description.desc: function unlistVesting(...) external isFreeze { — modifier blocks all callers before admin check at line 217

vulnerabilities.impact: All sellers with active listings at the time of a marketplace freeze have their vesting tokens locked indefinitely. The admin cannot perform emergency recovery without first unfreezing, which exposes the marketplace to the exact operations the freeze was meant to prevent. In the worst case, if the admin freezes due to a discovered exploit, unfreezing to recover tokens reopens the exploit window. The financial impact equals the total value of all listed vesting tokens at the time of freeze.

vulnerabilities.title: Missing discountPct Upper Bound Creates Unpurchasable Listings Trapping Seller Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: listVesting does not validate that discountPct < BASE (10000). If a seller sets discountPct >= BASE with FIX or LINEAR discount type, _getDiscountedPrice will underflow on (BASE - discountPct), reverting every purchase attempt. The seller's vesting tokens are trapped in the marketplace until they unlist (potentially paying a penalty fee).

description.desc: No require(discountPct < BASE) at listing creation; _getDiscountedPrice line 266: BASE - listing.discountPct underflows for discountPct >= 10000

vulnerabilities.impact: The defect has two main impacts: (1) Seller token trapping - vesting tokens are locked in VestingManager until the seller pays a penalty fee to unlist or waits for minListingDuration to pass. (2) Marketplace griefing - unpurchasable listings waste buyer gas and degrade marketplace usability. Additionally, values just below BASE (e.g., 9999) create near-zero prices that could enable economic exploitation.

vulnerabilities.title: PARTIAL Listing minPurchaseAmt Not Validated Against Listing Amount
vulnerabilities.summary: For PARTIAL listings, minPurchaseAmt is not validated to be <= _amount during listVesting. A seller can set minPurchaseAmt > _amount, creating a listing where the only valid purchase is _amount == listing.balance (the full remaining amount), effectively making it a SINGLE-type listing while bypassing SINGLE's explicit amount validation.

description.desc: Validation of minPurchaseAmt only for SINGLE type; PARTIAL type passes the require regardless of minPurchaseAmt value

vulnerabilities.impact: The defect has medium severity: (1) It allows creating PARTIAL listings that cannot function as intended, degrading user experience and marketplace utility. (2) The dust scenario can grief sellers by leaving small unsellable remainders that require exact-balance purchases or penalty-incurring delists. (3) No direct fund loss occurs since the `_amount == listing.balance` escape prevents permanent locking, but sellers may lose penalty fees when unlisting stuck dust. The issue is a clear violation of the expected invariant that PARTIAL listings should support partial purchases.

vulnerabilities.title: Penalty Fee Denomination Mismatch With 6-Decimal USDT Makes Early Delisting Impossible
vulnerabilities.summary: penaltyFee is initialized to `10 ether` (10 * 10^18) in MarketplaceSetting's constructor. If the configured USDT token has 6 decimals (standard on Ethereum, Base, Arbitrum), this represents 10 trillion USDT. The deployment script (DeploySecondSwap.s.sol) does not call setPenaltyFee after deployment. Early delisting becomes impossible for all sellers until admin manually fixes the fee.

description.desc: penaltyFee = 10 ether — uses 18-decimal denomination for a 6-decimal token
Affected function: unlistVesting

vulnerabilities.impact: All sellers who attempt early unlisting within minListingDuration (default 120 seconds) will have their transactions revert. Their vesting tokens remain locked in listings until the minimum duration passes or admin corrects the fee. While the minListingDuration is only 120 seconds (reducing impact), the fundamental code defect is real: the hardcoded default uses 18-decimal denomination for a 6-decimal token. Impact severity depends on whether admin notices and fixes before deployment to production, but the code itself is defective.

vulnerabilities.title: setMaxSellPercent Has No Upper Bound Allowing Token Issuer to Bypass Sell Restrictions
vulnerabilities.summary: VestingManager.setMaxSellPercent accepts any uint256 value without upper bound validation. The tokenIssuer can set maxSellPercent to values far exceeding BASE (10000), effectively allowing all original allocation holders to sell 100% of their vesting, bypassing the intended supply restriction mechanism.

description.desc: function setMaxSellPercent(address vesting, uint256 maxSellPercent) external — no require(maxSellPercent <= BASE)

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The missing validation allows the tokenIssuer to bypass the sell percentage restriction mechanism, undermining the protocol's secondary market supply controls. While this requires the tokenIssuer's action (semi-trusted role), it represents a code defect inconsistent with all other percentage parameters in the system. Additionally, extremely large values can cause DoS via overflow. The available() check prevents actual over-transfer of tokens but does not preserve the intended percentage-based restriction.

vulnerabilities.title: WhitelistDeployer is permissionless: any attacker can deploy arbitrary whitelist contracts (spoofing/private-sale confusion)
vulnerabilities.summary: SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.deployWhitelist() is external and permissionless. An attacker can deploy look-alike whitelist contracts with misleading parameters/events (e.g., same lotOwner) and potentially trick integrators/off-chain services that rely on emitted WhitelistCreated events from the deployer address rather than the actual listing.whitelist stored in Marketplace.

description.desc: external deployWhitelist() has no access control; emits WhitelistCreated for any caller

vulnerabilities.impact: On-chain: minimal direct impact - the Marketplace correctly stores and validates whitelist addresses from listings storage, not from events. Off-chain: moderate impact - if indexers/UI use deployer events as the source of truth for whitelist discovery (rather than Marketplace listing storage), attackers can create confusion about which whitelists are legitimate. Users may whitelist on wrong contracts, failing eligibility for actual private sales. Gas is wasted. The severity is MEDIUM because the on-chain protocol remains secure, but the missing access control is a real code defect that enables integration-level attacks.

vulnerabilities.title: Whitelist capacity cannot be reduced and is only increasable, enabling permanent griefing of private sales via pre-filling
vulnerabilities.summary: In SecondSwap_Whitelist, anyone can self-whitelist until totalWhitelist == maxWhitelist. The lotOwner can only increase maxWhitelist (cannot decrease or clear entries). An attacker (or group) can pre-fill a private whitelist to max, preventing intended participants from joining; the seller cannot remove malicious entries and must increase maxWhitelist, effectively diluting access control.

description.desc: setMaxWhitelist requires _maxWhitelist > maxWhitelist and > totalWhitelist; no removal mechanism exists

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. Private sale functionality is fundamentally broken against adversarial conditions. Sellers who rely on the whitelist for access control will find their private sales open to griefing. The seller's only recourses are: (1) cancel the listing (potentially paying a penalty fee), (2) increase maxWhitelist indefinitely (which the attacker can keep filling), or (3) accept that 'private' sales are not actually private. This affects the protocol's core value proposition for private/OTC sales.

vulnerabilities.title: Initialize functions do not validate critical addresses, enabling permanent bricking if initialized with wrong marketplaceSetting/manager/token
vulnerabilities.summary: SecondSwap_Marketplace.initialize() and SecondSwap_VestingManager.initialize() accept addresses without non-zero or contract checks. If initialized incorrectly (zero addresses or wrong contracts), core flows revert or become irreparably miswired (e.g., marketplaceSetting points to EOA causing isFreeze to revert; vestingManager has wrong s2Admin).

description.desc: initialize sets isTokenSupport[_token]=true and marketplaceSetting=_marketplaceSetting without require(_marketplaceSetting!=0) or code-size checks
Affected function: initialize(address)

vulnerabilities.impact: If triggered during initial deployment, all marketplace operations halt permanently. Existing listings cannot be purchased or unlisted. New listings cannot be created. For VestingManager, all admin functions become inaccessible, preventing marketplace wiring, deployer configuration, and fee management. Recovery requires proxy admin intervention via upgradeAndCall, which introduces operational complexity and downtime. The impact affects all protocol users.

vulnerabilities.title: Marketplace doesFunctionExist("decimals()") check is unreliable and can be bypassed by tokens with fallback returning success, causing pricing denominator misuse
vulnerabilities.summary: doesFunctionExist performs staticcall to the selector and considers success=true as 'function exists'. A token contract with a permissive fallback can return success for any selector (including decimals()), yet decimals() may not be implemented or may return malformed data. Subsequent IERC20Extended(token).decimals() call can revert or return attacker-controlled garbage if the ABI decode succeeds, corrupting baseAmount computation and potentially enabling mispriced listings/purchases or DoS.

description.desc: doesFunctionExist uses staticcall(abi.encodeWithSelector(selector)) and only checks success; then decimals() is called and used in 10**decimals denominator

vulnerabilities.impact: 1. DoS: Any vesting plan with a token returning decimals > 77 permanently blocks listings and purchases. 2. Mispricing: A token with spoofed decimals (e.g., 0 instead of 18) inflates baseAmount by 10^18x, making listings appear to have enormous value or rendering purchases impractical. 3. The defect exists in two code paths: listVesting (with unreliable doesFunctionExist check) and _handleTransfers (without any check). Even legitimate tokens with unusual decimals (> 77, though rare) would trigger the overflow.

vulnerabilities.title: Grantor releaseRate Miscalculation Enables Token Theft from Vesting Contract
vulnerabilities.summary: In transferVesting, the grantor's releaseRate is recalculated as totalAmount/numOfSteps instead of (totalAmount-amountClaimed)/(numOfSteps-stepsClaimed). This inflates the per-step claim rate, allowing a seller who has already claimed some steps to over-claim tokens belonging to other beneficiaries in intermediate steps before the final step reverts from underflow.

description.desc: grantorVesting.releaseRate = grantorVesting.totalAmount / numOfSteps — divides by total steps instead of remaining steps (numOfSteps - grantorVesting.stepsClaimed)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. The overclaim amount scales with: (a) the ratio of stepsClaimed to numOfSteps at transfer time, (b) the number of remaining intermediate steps. In worst case scenarios with many steps claimed before transfer, the overclaim can be a significant percentage of the remaining allocation. The stolen tokens come directly from the vesting contract's balance, making other beneficiaries insolvent - their claims will revert with insufficient balance. This can be repeated across multiple transfer-claim cycles for cumulative extraction.

vulnerabilities.title: VestingManager stepsClaimed Contamination Corrupts Buyer Vesting Schedules
vulnerabilities.summary: VestingManager acts as intermediary holder during marketplace trades. Its _vestings record accumulates a single stepsClaimed from the FIRST seller who lists on a given vesting plan. All subsequent buyers inherit this potentially stale stepsClaimed value regardless of the actual seller's progress, causing buyers to receive incorrect vesting schedules.

description.desc: _createVesting for existing beneficiary (VestingManager) only updates totalAmount and releaseRate, never updates stepsClaimed. First seller's stepsClaimed becomes permanent for all manager-intermediated transfers.
Affected function: listVesting

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: All marketplace-traded vesting allocations on any plan where multiple sellers have listed are affected. Buyers receive incorrect vesting schedules. In the acceleration direction, buyers can claim tokens earlier than intended, potentially draining the vesting contract of tokens belonging to other beneficiaries. In the deceleration direction, buyers are locked out of tokens they should be able to claim. The VestingManager's releaseRate is also incorrectly calculated because it uses the contaminated stepsClaimed, compounding the accounting errors. Combined with the separate grantor releaseRate bug (totalAmount / numOfSteps instead of remaining), this creates severe solvency risks for the vesting contract.

vulnerabilities.title: Referral Fee Calculated But Never Transferred to Referrer
vulnerabilities.summary: In _handleTransfers, referralFeeCost is computed and emitted in the Purchased event, but no actual token transfer to the referral address occurs. The entire buyerFeeTotal + sellerFeeTotal is sent to feeCollector, keeping the referral's share. The referral system is completely non-functional despite events suggesting otherwise.

description.desc: referralFeeCost computed at line 295-298, but feeCollectorTotal at line 300 = buyerFeeTotal + sellerFeeTotal (does not subtract referralFeeCost). No safeTransfer to _referral address exists.

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The referral incentive system is completely non-functional despite having full event infrastructure suggesting it works. Any referral tracking system built on the Purchased event's referralReward field will show incorrect data — it will report rewards being paid that were never actually transferred. The feeCollector accumulates all referral fees that should have been distributed. Over the lifetime of the marketplace, this represents 100% loss of all referral rewards.

vulnerabilities.title: Self-Service Whitelist Bypass Undermines Private Sale Restrictions
vulnerabilities.summary: The whitelistAddress() function has no access control — any user can call it to add themselves to any whitelist. Private listing protection is reduced to a first-come-first-served capacity limit rather than seller-curated access control. Any attacker can whitelist themselves and purchase from private listings intended for specific buyers.

description.desc: whitelistAddress() is external with no caller restriction — any address can whitelist itself up to maxWhitelist capacity
Affected function: spotPurchase

vulnerabilities.impact: All private listings on the SecondSwap marketplace are affected. The 'private sale' feature provides no meaningful access restriction — it only limits the number of participants (via maxWhitelist), not their identity. Sellers who create private listings expecting seller-curated access (e.g., for OTC deals at specific prices with known counterparties) will find their listings accessible to anyone. This undermines the core value proposition of private listings. If private listings offer discounted pricing compared to public markets, attackers gain arbitrage opportunities.

vulnerabilities.title: Disabling Sellable Status Does Not Pause Active Listings
vulnerabilities.summary: The setSellable(false) function is intended to halt trading for a specific vesting plan. However, spotPurchase does not check the sellable status, meaning existing listings remain fully active and purchasable.

description.desc: spotPurchase calls _validatePurchase which only checks listing.status == Status.LIST, ignoring vestingSettings[plan].sellable
Affected function: setSellable()

vulnerabilities.impact: The per-vesting-plan sellable control is a key risk management feature that allows administrators to selectively halt trading for specific plans without affecting the entire marketplace. Because this control is incomplete (only blocks new listings, not purchases of existing ones), it fails to provide the intended emergency halt capability. During a security incident affecting a specific vesting plan, attackers could purchase from any existing listing before the admin can either freeze the entire marketplace or manually unlist all affected listings. This defeats the granular risk management architecture.

vulnerabilities.title: Token Owner Assignment is Immutable and Restricts Multi-Token Issuers
vulnerabilities.summary: The setTokenOwner function maps an owner address to a token address and requires the mapping to be empty. This prevents an issuer from owning multiple tokens and prevents the admin from correcting mistakes.

description.desc: require(_tokenOwner[_owner] == address(0)); _tokenOwner[_owner] = token;

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect creates two operational limitations: (1) Each token owner address can only manage one token, forcing multi-token issuers to use separate wallets. (2) In a key compromise scenario, the admin cannot revoke or transfer token ownership, leaving the compromised key holder with permanent authority over vesting operations including the ability to arbitrarily transfer any user's vested tokens via VestingDeployer.transferVesting(). The proxy upgradeability provides an eventual escape hatch but not an immediate one.

vulnerabilities.title: Unbounded Array in createVestings Leads to Out-of-Gas DoS
vulnerabilities.summary: The createVestings function iterates over an unbounded array of beneficiaries, performing state updates and external token transfers for each. A large array will exceed the block gas limit.

description.desc: for loop over _beneficiaries.length calling _createVesting which does safeTransferFrom

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect is a missing bounds check that prevents operational failure. Impact is limited because: (1) only privileged callers are affected, (2) the workaround (smaller batches) is straightforward, (3) no funds are at risk. However, failed transactions waste gas, and the lack of any max length creates an unnecessary footgun for integrators and token issuers.

vulnerabilities.title: Changing Marketplace Settings Contract Desynchronizes Vesting Manager
vulnerabilities.summary: If the admin updates the marketplaceSetting address to a new contract that points to a different vestingManager, existing active listings will break because spotPurchase will attempt to call completePurchase on the new manager.

description.desc: marketplaceSetting = _marketplaceSetting; without state migration checks

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH. All actively listed vesting tokens become inaccessible through the marketplace. Sellers cannot recover their escrowed tokens via unlistVesting. The tokens are not permanently lost (they still exist in the old VestingManager), but there is no marketplace-level recovery mechanism. The admin would need to either revert the change or set up the old VestingManager's marketplace pointer to a rescue contract. During the desynchronization period, all marketplace trading is effectively halted for existing listings.

vulnerabilities.title: Implementation Contracts Missing _disableInitializers() Allow Direct Initialization
vulnerabilities.summary: SecondSwap_Marketplace, SecondSwap_VestingManager, and SecondSwap_VestingDeployer use OpenZeppelin's Initializable pattern but do not include constructors calling _disableInitializers(). The implementation contracts deployed behind TransparentUpgradeableProxy remain uninitialized. An attacker can call initialize() directly on the implementation contract, becoming admin. While Transparent proxy separates implementation state from proxy state, initialized implementation contracts are a known best-

description.desc: No constructor present; uses Initializable.initializer but never calls _disableInitializers() in a constructor
Affected function: initialize
Affected function: initialize

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity code defect. Under the current Transparent proxy architecture, the immediate impact is limited since proxy state is separate from implementation state. However: (1) It is a best-practice violation explicitly warned against by OpenZeppelin. (2) If the upgrade pattern changes to UUPS, the attacker-controlled implementation could be used to call selfdestruct (pre-Dencun) or manipulate upgrade authorization. (3) The implementation address being controllable by an attacker creates confusion and potential social engineering vectors. (4) If any other protocol contracts reference the implementation address directly instead of the proxy, the attacker gains control of those interactions.

vulnerabilities.title: stepDuration=0 in StepVesting permanently locks all deposited tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: When a token issuer deploys a vesting plan via VestingDeployer.deployVesting() where numOfSteps > (endTime - startTime), the constructor computes stepDuration = (endTime - startTime) / numOfSteps = 0 via integer truncation. All subsequent calls to claimable() divide elapsedTime by stepDuration (0), causing a permanent revert. No beneficiary can ever claim tokens from this vesting plan.

description.desc: Constructor: stepDuration = (_endTime - _startTime) / _numOfSteps; claimable(): currentStep = elapsedTime / stepDuration — division by zero when stepDuration=0
Affected function: deployVesting

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: All tokens deposited via createVesting/createVestings into a vesting plan with stepDuration=0 are permanently and irrecoverably locked. No beneficiary can ever claim. The contract has no admin rescue function, no emergency withdrawal, and stepDuration is immutable. The total value at risk equals the entire token allocation deposited into the broken vesting contract.

vulnerabilities.title: Admin toggle of setSellable resets tokenIssuer's maxSellPercent and custom fee overrides
vulnerabilities.summary: When a tokenIssuer sets maxSellPercent to 0 to prevent secondary market sales, the admin can override this by calling setSellable(vesting, false) then setSellable(vesting, true). The second call sees maxSellPercent==0 && sellable==true, triggering the default initialization block that resets maxSellPercent to 2000 (20%) and fees to -1 (global default). This unintended side effect allows the admin to circumvent tokenIssuer restrictions and reset any custom fee configuration.

description.desc: if (vestingSetting.maxSellPercent == 0 && vestingSetting.sellable) { vestingSetting.maxSellPercent = 2000; vestingSetting.buyerFee = -1; vestingSetting.sellerFee = -1; } — condition triggers when tokenIssuer explicitly set maxSellPercent=0

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is HIGH. This defect allows the admin to circumvent the tokenIssuer's explicit restriction on secondary market sales. Per the trust model, maxSellPercent is controlled by the tokenIssuer (enforced by the `require(SecondSwap_StepVesting(vesting).tokenIssuer() == msg.sender)` check in setMaxSellPercent). The setSellable side-effect bypasses this access control, enabling the admin to unilaterally re-enable selling at 20% despite the tokenIssuer's explicit 0% setting. Additionally, any custom per-plan fee overrides (set by admin via setBuyerFee/setSellerFee) are also reset to -1 (global defaults), causing unintended fee changes.

vulnerabilities.title: Marketplace freeze locks all listed vesting tokens with no emergency unlist path
vulnerabilities.summary: Both listVesting and unlistVesting in the Marketplace have the isFreeze modifier, which requires !isMarketplaceFreeze. When the admin freezes the marketplace, ALL listed tokens become inaccessible — sellers cannot unlist to recover their vesting allocations, and the admin cannot perform emergency unlisting since the modifier applies to all callers. VestingManager.unlistVesting is only callable by the marketplace (onlyMarketplace), so there is no alternative recovery path during freeze.

description.desc: isFreeze modifier on unlistVesting prevents all callers (including admin) from unlisting during freeze; VestingManager.unlistVesting has onlyMarketplace with no admin bypass
Affected function: unlistVesting
Affected function: setMarketplaceStatus()

vulnerabilities.impact: All active marketplace listings are frozen during a marketplace freeze. Sellers cannot recover their vesting allocations. The admin cannot perform emergency unlisting despite having explicit authorization in the function body. Time-sensitive vesting claims may be missed if underlying tokens continue vesting during the freeze period. On chains with mempools, the unfreeze-unlist-refreeze workaround is vulnerable to frontrunning by other users who could list, purchase, or exploit other issues during the brief unfreeze window.

vulnerabilities.title: VestingManager and VestingDeployer setAdmin missing zero-address check allows permanent admin lockout
vulnerabilities.summary: VestingManager.setAdmin() and VestingDeployer.setAdmin() accept address(0) as the new admin, unlike MarketplaceSetting.setS2Admin() which explicitly checks for zero address. Setting s2Admin to address(0) permanently locks all admin functions on these contracts: setMarketplace, setVestingDeployer, setSellable (admin path), setBuyerFee, setSellerFee on VestingManager, and setTokenOwner, setManager on VestingDeployer. This effectively bricks critical protocol infrastructure with no recovery path.

description.desc: function setAdmin(address _admin) external onlyAdmin { s2Admin = _admin; } — no require(_admin != address(0))
Affected function: setAdmin

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect permanently disables admin configurability on VestingManager or VestingDeployer if triggered. Affected operations: setMarketplace (cannot point to new marketplace), setVestingDeployer (cannot update deployer), setBuyerFee/setSellerFee (cannot adjust per-vesting fees), setSellable via admin path (cannot enable/disable vesting trading). Existing operations (marketplace purchases, claims, listings) continue to function with current settings. Proxy upgrade provides a recovery path but requires ProxyAdmin intervention.

vulnerabilities.title: Reentrancy in spotPurchase via Callback-Enabled Currency Token
vulnerabilities.summary: In spotPurchase, _handleTransfers executes external safeTransferFrom/safeTransfer calls to the currency token BEFORE listing.balance is decremented. If the currency token supports transfer callbacks (ERC-777, hooks), an attacker can reenter spotPurchase during the callback and purchase the same listing multiple times, extracting vesting tokens beyond the listing balance.

description.desc: spotPurchase calls _handleTransfers (external token transfers at lines 287-305) BEFORE updating listing.balance at line 326. No reentrancy guard present.

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect is real (CEI violation, no reentrancy guard). Practical exploitation is limited: (1) requires admin to add ERC-777 token, (2) Solidity 0.8 prevents buying beyond listing.balance, (3) each reentrant purchase is independently paid for. The defect could become more exploitable if future code changes alter the balance logic, or if cross-function reentrancy vectors emerge. The missing reentrancy guard is a standard security anti-pattern that should be remediated.

vulnerabilities.title: Division by Zero Blocks End-of-Vesting Transfers to New Beneficiaries
vulnerabilities.summary: In _createVesting, when a new beneficiary receives a transfer and numOfSteps == _stepsClaimed (all steps elapsed), releaseRate = amount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) causes division by zero. This prevents transferring vesting tokens to new addresses after the full vesting period, blocking both marketplace purchases and direct transfers at end-of-vesting.

description.desc: releaseRate: _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) — when _stepsClaimed == numOfSteps, divides by zero

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Users who fully claim their vesting and then receive additional tokens (via createVesting) cannot transfer those tokens to new addresses. This blocks marketplace listings for affected sellers (if VestingManager's totalAmount is 0 at listing time) and blocks direct off-market transfers to new beneficiaries. Tokens remain claimable by the user directly but cannot be redistributed. The inconsistency between the two branches in _createVesting is a clear code defect.
